title: No password reset flow
plain_english_explanation: If someone forgets their password they are permanently locked out of their account forever. There is no 'Forgot Password' button anywhere in the app. All their saved items, messages, and listing history are gone.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Sign up with email. 2. Sign out. 3. Try to sign back in but pretend to forget password. 4. Look for 'Forgot Password' anywhere on the sign-in screen. 4. It does not exist.
suggested_fix: Add sendPasswordResetEmail() in ApiService; add 'Forgot Password' link on SignInScreen; create PasswordResetScreen with email input and success/error feedback.

title: No email verification
plain_english_explanation: Anyone can create a fake account using an email address they don't own. Example: someone could make 100 accounts in minutes using random emails, flood the feed with fake listings, or harass sellers — no verification required.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Sign up using fake@anything.xyz. 2. Account created and fully functional. 3. No email sent. No verification required.
suggested_fix: Call sendEmailVerification() after signup; gate feed access on emailVerified == true; show verification prompt screen before allowing activity.

title: Seller phone numbers readable by any logged-in user
plain_english_explanation: Every seller's private phone number can be retrieved by any person who has an account in the app — without even pressing the 'Reveal Contact' button. Anyone with the app can bulk-download every seller's phone number from the database. This is a privacy violation that could cause regulatory issues.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Sign in with any account. 2. Use Firestore REST API or the Firebase console to query sellers/{id}/private/contact. 3. Phone number is returned with no restriction.
suggested_fix: Change Firestore rule for sellers/{sellerId}/private/contact from 'allow read: if isAuthenticated()' to 'allow read: if false'. All reads must go through the getSellerPhone Cloud Function which logs usage.

title: No message sending rate limit
plain_english_explanation: There is no limit on how many messages a user can send. A harasser can send thousands of messages per minute to a seller. Every message costs money in Firebase. The app has limits on photo uploads and listing creation — but zero protection on messages.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Open any chat conversation. 2. Write a script or tap send rapidly. 3. Hundreds or thousands of messages are accepted with no block or warning.
suggested_fix: Add checkMessageRateLimit Cloud Function (30 messages/minute per user). Call before sendMessage() in ApiService. Copy the existing checkPublishRateLimit pattern.

title: No way for users to delete their account
plain_english_explanation: Users cannot delete their own account or personal data. If someone wants to be removed from the platform — their profile, phone number, listings, and chats — they have to contact support and wait for a developer to do it manually in the backend.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Open the app. 2. Go to Profile, then Settings. 3. Search for any option to delete account or remove personal data. 4. Nothing exists.
suggested_fix: Add 'Delete Account' button in Settings screen with a confirmation step; call FirebaseAuth.instance.currentUser.delete() and trigger a Cloud Function to erase Firestore user data, listings, and conversations.

title: Deleted conversations leave hidden data in the database
plain_english_explanation: When a user deletes a chat, it disappears from the screen — but every individual message inside that chat stays permanently in the database, hidden and wasting storage. Like throwing away a filing cabinet but leaving all the papers inside the drawers.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Start a conversation and send several messages. 2. Delete the conversation from Messages screen. 3. Open Firebase Firestore console. 4. Check conversations/{id}/messages — subcollection still fully exists.
suggested_fix: Create a Cloud Function that triggers on conversation deletion and recursively deletes all messages inside the subcollection. Use firebase-admin batch delete.

title: Thumbnail preview images never expire
plain_english_explanation: When a seller uploads a listing photo, the app creates a small preview image with a link that expires in the year 2099 — practically never. If a listing is deleted, that preview image link still works forever. Anyone who saved the link can still see the deleted photo.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Upload a listing with images and publish. 2. Check the thumbnail_url stored in Firestore — it is a signed URL with expiry 01-01-2099. 3. Delete the listing. 4. Open the thumbnail_url in a browser — image still displays.
suggested_fix: Switch from getSignedUrl() with 2099 expiry to Firebase Storage download tokens or public download URLs with proper Storage rules. Update generateThumbnail Cloud Function.

title: App can crash if user navigates away during publishing
status_since_v1: STILL OPEN — CONFIRMED BY FLUTTER ANALYZE
plain_english_explanation: If a user taps the Publish button on a listing and then quickly taps the back button while photos are uploading, the app has a small chance of crashing. The code quality checker flags this in 4 specific places.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. On SellScreen, begin publishing a listing with large images. 2. While upload is in progress, tap the back button quickly. 3. Potential app crash due to code using a screen reference that no longer exists (BuildContext across async gap).
suggested_fix: Add 'if (!mounted) return;' guard checks before UI updates after async operations in sell_screen.dart (lines 561 and 617) and preferences_screen.dart (lines 261 and 264).

title: No SSL pinning — network traffic can be intercepted
plain_english_explanation: On public WiFi (café, airport, mall), a person with special tools could potentially sit between the phone and the internet and read data being sent back and forth. This is an advanced attack but becomes a concern as Wavy grows and processes more sensitive transactions.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Connect test device to a local proxy (e.g. mitmproxy). 2. Launch the app. 3. Firebase SDK network traffic may be visible and readable.
suggested_fix: Implement certificate pinning using SecurityContext or a plugin like flutter_security_advisory targeted at Firebase endpoints.

title: Developer test data tool left in production code
plain_english_explanation: There is a function called migrateDummyData() in the production app code that was originally used during development to fill the app with fake test products. It still exists and could accidentally be called, which would mix test listings into the real user feed.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Open lib/src/data/api_service.dart. 2. Search for migrateDummyData() — it is present and callable.
suggested_fix: Remove migrateDummyData() from ApiService entirely, or gate it behind a debug-only flag that evaluates to false in all production builds.

title: App download size is 81 MB — too large for local market
plain_english_explanation: The app file size is 81 MB. In Addis Ababa where many users are on metered or slow data connections, a large download creates friction at the point of first install. Typical Flutter apps are 15–30 MB when properly configured.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Build release APK: flutter build apk --release. 2. Check output size: ~81 MB in build/app/outputs/flutter-apk/app-release.apk.
suggested_fix: Build with --split-per-abi flag (reduces size ~50% by delivering only the code the user's phone needs). Add ProGuard/R8 rules. Audit asset bundle sizes.

title: Web login uses a public test security key
plain_english_explanation: The web version of the app uses a widely-known test key for reCAPTCHA (Google's bot protection). This test key is public, never blocks anything, and will make the web login completely unprotected against bots if deployed.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Open wavy/lib/main.dart line 34. 2. The reCAPTCHA provider key is 6LeIxAcTAAAAAJcZVRqyHh71UMIEGNQ_MXjiZKhI — Google's publicly documented test key.
suggested_fix: Replace with a real production reCAPTCHA v3 site key obtained from the Google reCAPTCHA admin console at https://www.google.com/recaptcha/admin

title: Chat conversations only show 5 messages at opening
plain_english_explanation: When a user opens a chat that has many messages, only the 5 most recent are shown initially. For active conversations this looks like most of the chat is missing. Users may think the app is broken.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Have a conversation with more than 10 messages. 2. Close and reopen the chat screen. 3. Only 5 messages are shown initially.
suggested_fix: Increase the initial load limit from 5 to 20-30 messages in the getMessages() API call in api_service.dart.

title: Feed shows listings the user has already swiped on
plain_english_explanation: When a user swipes left (passes) on a listing and then restarts the app, the same listing appears again in their feed. The app does not remember which items a user has already seen.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Swipe left on several listings. 2. Close the app completely. 3. Reopen and browse the feed. 4. Previously-seen listings reappear.
suggested_fix: Track swiped item IDs per user in a Firestore subcollection. On feed load, exclude previously-swiped items using a client-side or server-side NOT IN filter.

title: Amharic language missing some translated strings
plain_english_explanation: When the app is set to Amharic, some text still appears in English because not all words have been translated. The current system uses a patchwork approach rather than a proper translation framework.
technical_steps_to_reproduce: 1. Switch language to Amharic from Profile screen. 2. Navigate through the app. 3. Observe some labels and messages still appear in English.
suggested_fix: Migrate all inline ternary locale checks (e.g. locale == 'am' ? '...' : '...') to use the AppLocalizations.of(context).textKey pattern with proper ARB files for en and am.
